summary: Gruber declares the iPhone the greatest piece of consumer electronics ever made, arguing that while its individual features exist in other devices, the overall experience is unmatched. He reviews the iPhone 3G as an incremental but worthwhile upgrade, with 3G networking speeds far exceeding Apple's marketing claims. He examines the hardware tradeoffs — the plastic back is less attractive but less slippery, battery life suffers but per-page efficiency holds up. He closes with an Andy Warhol quote about Coca-Cola to make the point that the iPhone is a great equalizer: no amount of money can buy a better phone.
key_points: The iPhone's superiority lies not in its feature checklist but in the overall experience — software feel, hardware reduction, and integration.
iPhone OS prioritizes responsiveness and feel over visual fidelity, the opposite of Mac OS X's approach, and Gruber considers this the right tradeoff.
The iPhone 3G is a modest hardware iteration, but 3G networking alone — especially with tethering via NetShare — makes the upgrade worthwhile.
Battery life is the iPhone's single biggest shortcoming, driven by the fundamental tension between device size and capability.
The iPhone is a democratizing device: no amount of wealth can buy a better phone than what anyone can pocket today.
money_quotes: Let's just say it up front: the iPhone is the greatest piece of consumer electronics that has ever been made.
Everything Apple as a company has ever stood for, good and bad, was to get to the point where they could make this.
Mac OS X values the visual over the feel; iPhone OS is vice versa, and I prefer it.
The iPhone has significant and obvious shortcomings. But it is an order of magnitude better than anything that came before it.
There is something to be said for the fact that the phone with the strongest brand in the world has no visible branding whatsoever on its front face.
Apple sometimes seems to be the lone consumer electronics company that pays any attention at all to the tactile response of buttons.
A billionaire can buy homes, cars, clothes that the rest of us cannot afford. But he cannot buy a better phone, at any price, than the iPhone that you can have in your pocket today.
key_insight: The iPhone's greatness lies not in any single feature but in Apple's relentless prioritization of experience and feel, making it both the best consumer electronics device ever made and one equally available to everyone.
